title: SEGMENT 4 — The diagnostic odyssey, panel testing, and why one mutation was missing

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — The diagnostic odyssey, panel testing, and why one mutation was missing
I want to spend this segment on a phenomenon that you will not have heard much about, but that is utterly central to the experience of families dealing with rare genetic disease.
It is called the diagnostic odyssey.
Here's what a diagnostic odyssey looks like.
A child starts showing symptoms — say, the foot turning inward, the early vision changes, the loss of vocabulary I described in Segment 2.
The parents take the child to the pediatrician.
The pediatrician sees something is wrong, but doesn't know what, and refers the child to a specialist — maybe a neurologist.

The neurologist runs some tests, sees something is still wrong, and refers the child to another specialist — maybe a geneticist, maybe a metabolic disease specialist, maybe an ophthalmologist if the vision is involved.
Each specialist orders their own tests.
Each set of tests takes weeks or months to come back.
Some come back normal.
Some come back ambiguous.
Some come back suggesting a particular disease, which then requires confirmation testing, which takes more weeks or months.
Meanwhile the child is getting worse.

Meanwhile the family is making round-trip drives to academic medical centers, paying out of pocket for tests insurance won't cover, getting on waiting lists for the next specialist, and absorbing the staggering psychological weight of not knowing what is happening to their child or why.
In the rare-disease community, the typical diagnostic odyssey lasts somewhere between five and ten years.
Some families never get a diagnosis at all.
Many families see twenty or thirty specialists before someone finally lands on the right test or the right gene.
The journey is exhausting, expensive, and — most cruelly — usually does not, even when it ends in a diagnosis, lead to any treatment, because for most rare diseases there is no treatment to offer.
The diagnosis is just a name.
A label.
An explanation.
Sometimes that name itself is a kind of relief, because not-knowing is worse than knowing, but the label rarely changes the trajectory of the disease.
Mila's family was, by the standards of the rare-disease community, comparatively lucky in the speed of their diagnosis.
The clinical picture — the early-childhood onset, the vision loss, the seizures, the regression — pointed Boston Children's neurologists toward Batten disease relatively quickly.
The skin biopsy showing fingerprint inclusions confirmed it.

And the gene panel identified, within MFSD8, the gene for the CLN7 subtype, one specific mutation: a single-letter change at position 1102 in the coding sequence, where a G — guanine — had been replaced by a C — cytosine.
We write that as c.1102G>C.
This is a known pathogenic mutation.
It has been seen in other CLN7 patients.
It alters a single amino acid in the protein and renders that protein non-functional.
So one of Mila's two MFSD8 copies was definitively, identifiably broken.
But — and here is the puzzle — the other copy looked normal.
The panel sequenced every exon of MFSD8 on the second copy, and every exon was wild-type.
Every single one of the protein-coding stretches of the gene, on her second copy, was indistinguishable from a healthy person's.
Yet she clearly had the disease.
The skin biopsy was unambiguous.
The clinical course was textbook.
She could not have CLN7 with only one broken copy of MFSD8 — recessive diseases don't work that way.
The second mutation had to exist.
The panel was simply not finding it.
This is a moment to pause and explain why a gene panel can miss a mutation, because the explanation tells you something deep about how genetic testing actually works in clinical medicine.
Here is the analogy I want you to use.
A gene panel is like checking a list of usual suspects.
You have a crime, you have a list of people who fit the profile, you go down the list, you interview each one in turn, and you see whether any of them confess or have alibis that don't hold up.
It's efficient.
It's cheap.

It works well most of the time because the usual suspects really are usual — most pathogenic mutations in known genes fall in well-characterized parts of those genes, and a panel is designed to look exactly where mutations are commonly found.
But what does a panel actually sequence?

Almost all clinical gene panels sequence only the exons of the targeted genes — the protein-coding stretches — plus a small amount of the immediately flanking sequence at the boundaries between exons.
They do not sequence the introns — the non-coding stretches in between exons, which get spliced out during the production of the mature messenger RNA.
And they do not sequence anything outside the gene itself — none of the regulatory regions, none of the regions far upstream or downstream, and crucially, none of the intronic interior.
For most patients, that is fine, because most pathogenic mutations really are in the exons.
But not all of them.
Sometimes a mutation hides somewhere a panel does not look.

And when that happens, the panel comes back with a partial answer — like the answer for Mila — and the clinician has no way of telling, just from the panel result, whether there is a second mutation that exists but was missed, or whether the patient really does only have one mutation and therefore must have a non-recessive form of the disease, or some other explanation entirely.
So Mila's family was, at this point, in clinical limbo.
The diagnosis was clear.
The first mutation was identified.
The second mutation was missing.
And without finding the second mutation, no one could be certain enough about the precise molecular pathology to even speculate about what kind of treatment might help.
The path forward required going beyond the panel.
It required reading the entire genome — every base of her DNA, exons and introns and intergenic stretches alike — and looking for whatever the panel had missed.
That brings us to whole-genome sequencing.
